pages.text: Inﬂuence of the amount of mixed recycled aggregates on the properties of concrete for non-structural use Benito Mas, Antoni Cladera ⇑, Teodoro del Olmo, Francisco Pitarch Department of Physics, University of the Balearic Islands, Spain article info Article history: Received 23 June 2010 Received in revised form 10 June 2011 Accepted 18 June 2011 Available online 30 July 2011 Keywords: Sustainable development Building demolition Aggregate Recycled concrete Mechanical properties abstract This article presents the results of an experimental campaign on the inﬂuence of replacing natural aggre- gate with mixed recycled aggregate (MRA) in the mix design of non-structural concrete. Mixed recycled aggregates are those that come from construction and demolition waste treatment and include concrete waste, unbound aggregates and ceramic products as their predominant components. Speciﬁcally, a mix design suitable for manufacturing low-strength blinding concrete (15 MPa), medium-strength blinding/ backﬁll concrete (25 MPa) and non-structural concrete for high-resistance precast elements (65 MPa) is proposed. According to the properties of mixed recycled aggregates, the conclusion drawn is that it is feasible to substitute about 40% of coarse aggregate in such a way that the resulting coarse aggregate mix complies with all the regulatory requirements analysed. The higher absorption capacity of mixed recycled aggregates means that special precautions in the mix design are required, since consistency may be affected. In a hardened state, the compressive, tensile and ﬂexural strength properties decrease when the proportion of MRA increases. This decrease is proportionately higher when the concrete’s aver- age compressive strength increases. At 90 days, the reduction in average compressive strength is less than 15% for replacements around 20% of the coarse aggregate. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Each year, the construction sector – including several related industries such as ceramic products and other construction mate- rials ﬁrms – generates a very signiﬁcant amount of construction and demolition waste (CDW). The environmental problem posed by these wastes stems not only from their growing volume, but also from their treatment and disposal. The need to preserve natural resources is particularly important on the island of Mallorca, given the limitations involved in the shortage of land and importance of the tourism industry [1]. Taken together, these factors have led to the need to consider implement- ing an integrated waste management system, which materialised in 2006, in order to manage 100% of the construction and demoli- tion waste generated on the island [2]. Waste from industries such as precast concrete or ceramic manufacturing plants, non-selective demolitions and public works is treated together in this system and thus, it includes high percentages of components that do not come from concrete, such as brick, ceramic tile and others. The possibility of reusing recycled concrete aggregates to manu- facture structural and non-structural concrete has been studied in depth [3–8] . Research has also examined the possible use of recycled ceramic aggregates in manufacturing non-structural concrete[9–11] . However, given the island’s peculiar geographical nature and production model, the public model established in Mallorca pro- duces another type of recycled aggregate known as mixed recycled aggregate (MRA). Because of

which causes workability to decline; the w/c ratio in the paste will also be lower than desired. In their experimental phases, some studies have solved this problem by pre-saturating the aggregates before mixing. This is the case of reference [6],i n which recycled coarse aggregates were wetted by a sprinkler system the day before they were used and were covered by a plastic sheet in order to maintain their humidity. A recommended level of humidity could be 80% of the total absorption capacity, but recycled aggregates should not be saturated, as that would probably result in the failure of an effective interfacial transition zone between the saturated recycled coarse aggregates and the new cement paste. Other researchers, such as Gonzalez-Fonteboa et al. [24], decided to dry the aggregates using electric resistors to make it easier to control the consistency of the mixes. In the present study, however, MRA absorption was corrected by adding water in the mixer itself, taking into account the real moisture of the aggregates, thus sim- plifying the process for possible industrial applications. The sequence of adding the different components of the mix proportion in the mixer, shown in Fig. 8, is similar to the one proposed in [25]. To determine the absorption to be taken into account when compensating for this in the mixing process, it was decided to use absorption at 2 h as a reference (Fig. 5); this is the average open time of the cements used in the various campaigns, extracted from the manufacturers’ speciﬁcations. This is equal approximately 80% of the total absorption of the AMR. The decision to consider the absorption at 2 h was based on the experience of the research team, which had seen a high loss of workability when working the AMR in preliminary campaigns. However, several authors working with recycled concrete aggregates, such as Evangelista and Brito [3], consider that during the 10–30 min mixing period, the water absorption stabi- lizes, reaching around 50% of its maximum capacity. The reason is that the recycled aggregates absorption was signiﬁcantly reduced after the binder was introduced in the mixture, because it seals the aggregates pores, limiting water exchanges. For the AMR used in this study, the water absorbed during a period of 30 min was around 75% of the total absorption. 4.2.3. Testing In the case of Series I, compressive strength tests were obtained on the basis of test cylinders measuring 150 mm in diameter and 300 mm high; the results for Ser- ies II and Series III were obtained from prismatic specimens measuring 150 mm. Different cylinders and cube specimens were used because of a change in the lab- oratory procedure for environmental and workplace health and safety reasons. In any case, since the comparisons were conducted within the same series, the results were not affected. Tested specimens were subjected to wet curing until the day of testing and the procedure followed was the one deﬁned in UNE-EN 12390-3 [26]. Two or three specimens were used for each concrete mix and each age of testing. For ﬂexural tensile strength, two 15 /C2 15 /C2 60 cm or 10 /C2 10 /C2 40 cm (Series III) wet-cured prims were tested for each concrete mix, according to UNE-EN 12390-5 [27]. The results were corrected to take into account the different size of the specimens [18]. The penetration tests and split tensile strength used three 150 mm diame- ter /C2 300 mm tall wet-cured cylinders, according to UNE-EN 12390-8 [28] and UNE

value established in the Los Angeles test, absorption index and to- tal sulphate and acid-soluble sulphate contents. Earlier results obtained when using the 0/8 mm fraction to manufacture concrete meant the rejection of its use in this preli- minary stage [33]. This conclusion is consistent with the results obtained in recycled concrete with substitutions of recycled aggre- gate from concrete [7] [24]. However, the use of the ﬁne fraction of recycled concrete aggregates is encouraged by other authors, e.g., in [3]. Its use should probably not be ruled out in the future, although more research is still needed. As for coarse aggregate, it should be recalled that the current Standard EHE [18] allows the use of aggregates from recycled con- crete structures with a Los Angeles wear index of up to 50% in cases when previous experiences exist. Therefore, these aggregates could eventually be used. In addition, by raising the substitution percent- ages and considering the aggregates that were used (Los Angeles natural aggregate index equal to 31 and Los Angeles equal to 44 for MRA), recycled aggregate could eventually replace 69% of the natural coarse aggregate, so that the Los Angeles index for the aggregates mix would equal 40, this index having nearly linear behaviour [8]. The limiting value of the absorption index was also not com- plied with, with the recycled concrete aggregates exceeding the maximum value of 7%. This is undoubtedly due to the existence of a signiﬁcant percentage in the MRA’s composition of ceramic and sandstone products, the latter being a very porous local stone. However, if proper account is taken of this increased absorption, incompliance with this limitation should not restrict the use of MRA, since today, e.g., the use of light aggregates with even greater absorption capacities is common. Furthermore, for the aggregates used in this campaign (maximum recycled aggregate absorption of 7.96% and natural gravel with an absorption value of 0.91), up to 58% of MRA could be added, if the aim is to limit the coarse aggregate mix’s absorption to below the 5% limit [18]. In any case, RILEM’s ‘‘Speciﬁcations for concrete with recycled aggregates’’ [23] limit the maximum water absorption to 20% for Type I aggregates (originating from masonry rubble) and to 10% for Type II aggre- gates (originating from concrete rubble). The AMR tested in this re- search complies with this RILEM requirement, although they are not Type I or Type II aggregates. The most restrictive limitation that was observed for the use of MRA was the amount of acid-soluble sulphates and total sulphate content. Owing to the material’s intrinsic heterogeneity, these val- ues in the three samples used presented variable results; thus, a long-term campaign needs to be conducted to limit their variabil- ity. The sulphates present in the cement paste, now combined dur- ing hydration, will not cause problems in the new concrete, but it will be necessary to avoid the presence of impurities such as gyp- sum, which could cause the concrete to expand. Establishing a lim- it to the content of total sulphate instead of acid-soluble sulphates, would be working on the safe side, and the recommendations on recycled aggregate are oriented in this direction [34]. In the absence of a technological solution to reduce the pres- ence of soluble sulphates, limiting the substitution of natural aggregate by mixed recycled aggregates was proposed so that the resulting aggregate mix veriﬁ

Workability was corrected in Series II and III, in all cases by increasing the content of superplasticizing admixture when increasing the percentage of substitution. It was observed that workability increased in 20% substitutions when the superplasti- cizing admixture was used; thus, less admixture should be enough. However, in 40% substitutions, despite having added more water to the mix to correct for absorption, workability remained similar to that of the reference concretes, thanks to the increase in the super- plasticizing admixture. A relatively high variability in the slump re- sults can be seen, which reﬂects the difﬁculty of working with aggregates with a high heterogenity, high absorption values and a poor shape index. 5.2. Saturated bulk density Fig. 10 shows how the saturated bulk density in the concretes fell [32] as the MRA substitution rate rose. This is because the MRA’s speciﬁc dry gravity – 2.21 Mg/m 3 for the aggregates in Ser- ies I and 2.12 for those used in Series II and III – is lower than that of natural aggregates at 2.8 Mg/m 3. The decline is gradual and practically linear in all cases. 5.3. Compressive strength Table 6 presents the results obtained in the compression tests [26]. Series III, with very high compressive strength compared with the other series studied, presented the highest % of reduction when incorporating MRA, reaching 32% reduction at 90 days for a 40% addition of MRA. This reduction is much higher than in the two other series. It should be stressed that in all cases, the percentage of reduction in compressive strength at 90 days was lower than at 7 and 28 days, which reveals that adding MRA delayed harden- ing. This observation agrees with ﬁndings in [3], in which the ref- erence concrete’s resistance almost stabilized after 28 days of age in contrast to the compressive strength of the concrete mixes made with ﬁne recycled aggregates, which continued to increase after that age. This result corroborates the assumption that there is non-hydrated cement mixed with the ﬁne recycled concrete aggre- gates, which contributes to the overall resistance [3]. However, this observation contrasts with those by other authors, as in reference [6], which states that the increment of compression strength in conventional concrete and recycled concrete is similar for substitu- tions of up to 25%. When the percentage of recycled concrete aggregate employed in the mix was increased, the increase in com- pressive strength measured at 6 months was lower. 5.4. Tensile strength Fig. 11 and Table 7 show the results of tensile strength for the different mix proportions. strength using the ﬂexural test [27] and splitting tensile strength using the Brazilian test were obtained [29]. A general reduction in tensile strength was detected when the MRA was added, with the exception of II-20%. No reasonable expla- nation was found for the behaviour of this concrete mix, as its com- pressive strength was lower than the strength of the reference concrete. As with compressive strength, the maximum reduction of concrete took place in Series III. While it is difﬁcult to generalise with the available information, the % of reduction in compressive and tensile strength at 28 days is similar, although with much more variability in the latter case. These results agree with those found for concretes made with ﬁne recycled aggregates [3], justi- ﬁed because of the more porous structure of the recycled aggre- gates, and with results of replacing limestone aggregates with ceramic aggregates [9]. However, other researchers report that the inclusion of concrete recycled aggregates has no particular impact on tensile splitting strength [24] or that the recycled aggre- gates concretes have more splitting tensile strength than those of the conventional concretes, except for the 100% replacement [6]. 5.5. Water penetration under pressure Fig. 12 shows the evolution of average and maximum penetra- tion according to series analysed and percentage of MRA substi- tuted [28]. With respect to Series I, it is noteworthy that the water penetra- tion under pressure showed very high values, which is related to the high w/c ratio and strength class of the cement used. For Series II and III with higher cement strengths, more closely adjusted val- ues were obtained, permeability increasing in an almost linear manner with the% of substitution. The MRA used in this research contains around 20–30% of cera- mic materials. As Correia et al. [11] pointed out for concrete with ceramic aggregates, the durability of this type of concrete may turn out to be its major insufﬁciency, since water absorption increases signiﬁcantly with the proportion of ceramic aggregates in the con- crete mix. This may not be restrictive for non-reinforced elements, but for reinforced concrete, the high permeability may be danger- ous in aggressive environments. Representation of th

in material treated as of the second half of 2008, which accelerated during 2009. The total amount of waste treated by the management system each year [15] amounted to 567,145 tonnes in 2007, 467,245 ton- nes in 2008 and 346,913 tonnes in 2009. This declining trend is linked to the current crisis, which has been affecting the entire economy and, in particular, the construction sector since mid-2008. 3. Properties of mixed recycled aggregates The MRA for concrete production were taken from the treat- ment plant together with the samples for testing. The procedure deﬁned in UNE-EN 932-1:1997 [16] was followed. Due to the MRA’s non-homogeneous manufacturing process, which is strongly dependent on the material entering the treatment plant every day, the representativeness of each sample is limited to the day of collection. The classiﬁcation test for the constituents of recycled coarse aggregate was performed according to UNE-EN 933-11:2009 [17], which is used to determine recycled aggregate type. Table 1 shows the results of the test. Material from Sample 3 was tested twice: ﬁrst, the material was tested as it was taken from the treatment plant and second, it was tested after a second sieving, using only the 8/20 fraction. Table 2 presents the series of tests performed and results ob- tained from three recycled aggregate samples collected between 2007 and 2009. The 8/40 mm fraction from Samples 2 and 3 were used to manufacture concrete; speciﬁcally, Sample 2 was used in Series I and Sample 3 in Series II and III. It was decided not to use the 0/8 mm fraction to manufacture concrete in this initial experimental phase, although noting the results of the characterisation for future research was considered of interest. No separate test were conducted for the 8/20 fraction of Sample 3. The main requirements of Standard EHE-08 [18] which were not satisﬁed are: – Total sulphate content, expressed as S%: This limitation is related to possible alterations in setting and hardening, loss of strength and substantially diminished durability [19]. – Los Angeles abrasion test: It can be taken into account for this property that Standard EHE-08 [18] extends the indicated limit to 50 for aggregates whose previous experience in use is avail- able. The results are slightly worse than those found by Vegas et al. [20], who point out that mixed recycled aggregates with ceramic percentages of between 20% and 30% are shown to have fragmentation resistance values of between 35 and 40 in most cases. Moreover, they state that the ceramic material is the fraction with the greatest inﬂuence on recycled aggregate wearing and fragmentation, showing a linear correlation. The slightly worse results obtained in this study are probably due to the presence in the unbound aggregates of sandstone, a por- ous local stone very popular in traditional construction. It accounts, e.g., for 13% of the total MRA composition in Sample 3( Table 3 ). – Absorption: according to Standard EHE-08 [18], recycled con- crete that contains no more than 20% recycled aggregate from crushed concrete should have an absorption index of no more than 7%. Additionally, the absorption index for natural coarse aggregate must be no higher than 4.5%. In recycled concrete with more than 20% recycled aggregate concrete, the absorption index for the combination of recycled and natural coarse aggre- gate must not surpass 5%. – Acid-soluble sulphates: this limitation is related to a reaction known as sulphate att

5.6. Abrasion test The abrasion test was conducted for the concretes in Series II and III [30]. This test consisted in applying an abrasion cycle with a disc at a constant pressure ( Fig. 13) and a certain ﬂow of abrasive material, in this case corundum (aluminium oxide). After applying the cycle, the sizes of the tracks left were evaluated. Fig. 14 shows the evolution of the abrasion tests for Series II and III. It is noted that for the samples tested, the size of the tracks in the mix with 20% MRA remained practically the same with respect to the reference concrete. For the concrete with a 40% substitution, the tracks increased slightly, which is related to lower abrasion strength. During the course of this test, the presence of ceramic material was seen to cause a change in the behaviour of wear in contrast to abrasion. When the wear disc came into contact with ceramic material, the disc pivoted from the side where the ceramic material was found towards the side where there was no ceramic material. This behaviour is related to the extremely hard composi- tion of ceramic products, which despite being more fragile than natural aggregates, have harder surface characteristics, conﬁrming the behaviour observed in Ref. [9] for concretes with substitutions of recycled ceramic aggregates. 6. Discussion of results 6.1.
